prompt:
Passage 1
A regional hardware supplier recently introduced a barcode system for managing returned products. Before the change, employees recorded returns by hand on paper forms. The forms were later reviewed by office staff and entered into the inventory system. This sometimes caused delays, especially when handwriting was unclear or product numbers were missing.
Under the new system, employees scan the product barcode at the return counter. The system records the item number, return date, reason for return, and whether the item can be restocked. Managers can review return patterns more quickly and identify products that may have repeated quality issues.
Although the system required employees to learn a new process, supervisors report that return records are now more complete and easier to search.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: A barcode system improved how the company manages product returns.
choices.B: The company stopped accepting returned products.
choices.C: Paper forms are required for all inventory records.
choices.D: Customers now enter all return information themselves.

explanation: The passage focuses on how the barcode system improved product return records by making them more complete and easier to search.

prompt:
Passage 2
NOTICE
Beginning April 15, employees entering the assembly area must wear the updated visitor-style identification badge if they are not normally assigned to that department. The badge must be obtained from the front office and returned before leaving the building.
The updated badge procedure is intended to help supervisors quickly identify who is authorized to be in the assembly area. It will also improve emergency headcounts if the building must be evacuated.
Employees who regularly work in assembly will continue using their standard department badges.

What is the purpose of the notice?

choices.A: To explain a new payroll process
choices.B: To announce an updated badge procedure for the assembly area
choices.C: To cancel access to the assembly area
choices.D: To introduce a new assembly machine

explanation: The notice announces an updated badge procedure for employees entering the assembly area who are not normally assigned there.

prompt:
Passage 3
Many workplaces use training records to document which employees have completed required instruction. These records may include the employee’s name, the course completed, the date of completion, and when refresher training is due.
Training records help organizations confirm that employees are prepared for certain tasks. For example, before assigning a worker to operate specific equipment, a supervisor may check whether the employee has completed the required training. Records can also help companies schedule refresher sessions before previous training becomes outdated.
Accurate records are important because missing or incomplete information can create confusion. If a record does not show whether training was completed, supervisors may need to delay an assignment until the information is verified.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Training records are used only for new employees.
choices.B: Training records help organizations track completed instruction and plan assignments.
choices.C: Training records replace all workplace instruction.

explanation: The passage explains that training records document completed instruction and help supervisors make assignment decisions.

prompt:
Passage 4
Before widespread use of electric street lighting, many cities depended on gas lamps or oil lamps to light public streets. These systems required workers to light and maintain lamps regularly. Illumination was often limited, and some streets remained dark or difficult to travel at night.
Electric streetlights changed public lighting by providing brighter and more reliable illumination. Streets became easier to navigate after dark, and businesses could operate with improved visibility during evening hours. Electric lighting also reduced the amount of labor required to keep street lamps in service.
Over time, cities continued improving streetlight systems with better bulbs, automatic controls, and more efficient equipment. Modern street lighting remains an important part of public safety and transportation planning.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Cities stopped using public lighting after electricity became available.
choices.B: Electric street lighting improved public visibility and reduced maintenance demands.
choices.C: Gas lamps were brighter than all electric streetlights.
choices.D: Street lighting is unrelated to public safety.

explanation: The passage describes how electric street lighting improved visibility, reduced labor, and remained important for public safety and transportation.

prompt:
Passage 5
A large construction company recently reviewed how tools and materials are delivered to job sites. Previously, each crew requested supplies independently. This sometimes resulted in multiple deliveries to the same site in one day, while other crews waited for items that were not available.
The company created a centralized delivery request process. Crew leaders now submit requests by noon for the following workday. A logistics coordinator reviews the requests, combines deliveries when possible, and confirms which items are available before trucks are loaded.
Managers report that the new process has reduced unnecessary trips and made supply planning more predictable. Some crews initially found the deadline inconvenient, but most agreed that receiving more accurate delivery information helped them plan their work.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: The company stopped delivering tools to job sites.
choices.B: Independent crew requests were always efficient.
choices.C: A centralized request process improved job-site supply deliveries.
choices.D: Crews now purchase all materials themselves.

explanation: The passage focuses on how a centralized delivery request process reduced unnecessary trips and improved supply planning.

prompt:
Passage 6
Many companies use energy audits to evaluate how buildings use electricity, heating, cooling, and water. During an audit, specialists may inspect lighting, heating systems, insulation, windows, equipment, and operating schedules.
The purpose of an energy audit is to identify where resources are being wasted and where improvements may save money. For example, an audit may find that older lighting uses more electricity than newer fixtures or that heating is running in areas that are rarely occupied.
Not every recommendation requires major construction. Some improvements may involve adjusting schedules, repairing leaks, sealing gaps, or replacing outdated equipment. Organizations often use audit results to decide which changes are most practical and cost-effective.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Energy audits help organizations identify waste and practical improvements.
choices.B: Energy audits are used only to inspect employee performance.
choices.C: Buildings do not require heating or cooling.
choices.D: Specialists avoid reviewing equipment during audits.

explanation: The passage explains that energy audits identify waste and help organizations choose practical, cost-effective improvements.

prompt:
Passage 7
Many communities maintain public information signs in parks, transit centers, and municipal buildings. These signs may provide directions, safety reminders, event notices, maps, or instructions for using public services.
Clear signs can reduce confusion and help visitors find what they need without asking staff for assistance. However, signs must be placed where people can see them and written in language that is easy to understand. A sign with too much information or unclear wording may be ignored.
Local agencies often review public signs when services change, buildings are renovated, or visitors report confusion. Updating signs can be a simple way to improve public access to information.

What is the passage mainly about?

choices.A: How public information signs help people when they are clear and well placed
choices.B: Why communities should remove all public signs
choices.C: How parks are built
choices.D: Why visitors should avoid municipal buildings

explanation: The passage focuses on the usefulness of public signs when they are clear, visible, and updated.

prompt:
Passage 8
An apprenticeship preparation program recently added short reading exercises to its weekly math sessions. Instructors made the change after noticing that some students could solve calculations correctly but struggled when math problems were written as workplace word problems.
The new exercises ask students to identify important information, ignore unnecessary details, and determine what a question is asking before calculating. Instructors also model how to reread a problem and underline key numbers or conditions.
Early classroom results suggest that students are becoming more confident with word problems. Program staff plan to continue the exercises and compare quiz results over the next several months.

Why did the program add reading exercises to math sessions?

choices.A: Some students struggled with workplace word problems.
choices.B: Students no longer needed math instruction.
choices.C: Instructors wanted to remove all calculations.
choices.D: Weekly math sessions were being canceled.

explanation: The program added reading exercises because some students could calculate but struggled with workplace word problems.

choices.D: Instructors should avoid explaining problem-solving strategies.

explanation: The passage shows that reading strategies, such as identifying key information and rereading, can help students solve math word problems.
